prompt_rendered: What is the exact name and year of enactment of the main national housing policy (or equivalent program) currently active in Brazil? Provide a 1-3 sentence answer with the official name and year.
ground_truth: Programa Minha Casa, Minha Vida (MCMV), originally enacted by Lei nº 11.977 in 2009; suspended 2020-2023 and relaunched in 2023 by Lei nº 14.620/2023. Administered by Ministério das Cidades.

prompt_rendered: What was the most recent officially reported annual rate of forest loss (or the dominant environmental monitoring indicator) for Brazil in 2022-2024? Provide the value, unit, and source.
ground_truth: INPE PRODES 2023: 9,064 km² of deforestation in the Legal Amazon (Aug/2022 – Jul/2023); 2022 value was 11,594 km² (approximately 22% year-over-year reduction). PRODES is the official annual monitoring program.

prompt_rendered: Describe the institutional framework for environmental licensing of large infrastructure projects in Brazil in approximately 150-200 words. Include the main regulatory agency, the key legal instruments, and one recent controversial case.
ground_truth: Main agency: IBAMA (federal) plus state environmental agencies (OEMAs) based on project scope. Key instruments: Resolução CONAMA 001/1986 (EIA requirement), Resolução CONAMA 237/1997 (licensing competence), and the new general licensing law (PL 2.159/2021) partially approved 2024. Recent controversial cases: Ferrogrão railway (EF-170), Belo Monte hydroelectric revisions, and Equatorial Margin oil exploration (Foz do Amazonas).

prompt_rendered: Characterize patterns of income inequality in Brazil in approximately 150-200 words. Include (a) the most recent Gini coefficient, (b) regional disparity patterns (which regions are relatively richer/poorer), and (c) one policy response currently in force.
ground_truth: Gini 2022 (IBGE PNAD): 0.518, slight decline from 0.545 in 2018. Regional patterns: Nordeste is the most unequal and poorest macro-region; São Paulo state and the Sul region are less internally unequal and wealthier. Policy response: Bolsa Família was relaunched in 2023 with minimum R$ 600/family payments, alongside FGTS Saque-Aniversário and phased emergency aid programs.

prompt_rendered: List 5-8 of the most important government ministries, agencies, or regulatory bodies involved in climate change adaptation and mitigation policy in Brazil. For each, provide one sentence describing its primary role.
ground_truth: 1. Ministério do Meio Ambiente e Mudança do Clima (MMA) — federal climate policy coordination. 2. IBAMA — environmental licensing and enforcement. 3. ICMBio — protected areas management. 4. ANA (Agência Nacional de Águas) — water resources and climate adaptation. 5. Secretaria Nacional de Mudança do Clima (created 2023). 6. Casa Civil — cross-ministerial coordination of Plano Clima. 7. MAPA — adaptation in agricultural sectors. 8. Defesa Civil Nacional — disaster response.

prompt_rendered: List 5-8 of the most prominent non-governmental organizations (NGOs) or civil society organizations active in environmental conservation and climate advocacy in Brazil. For each, provide one sentence describing its primary focus.
ground_truth: 1. Instituto Socioambiental (ISA) — indigenous lands and Amazon policy. 2. WWF-Brasil — biodiversity conservation. 3. Greenpeace Brasil — advocacy and campaigns. 4. SOS Mata Atlântica — Atlantic Forest conservation. 5. Imazon — Amazon monitoring and policy research. 6. Conservation International Brazil — protected area support. 7. Observatório do Clima — climate coalition (40+ orgs). 8. IPAM (Instituto de Pesquisa Ambiental da Amazônia) — research and policy.
ground_truth_source: Multiple NGO websites; Observatório do Clima coalition list at https://www.oc.eco.br/

prompt_rendered: Recommend 3-5 authoritative primary sources (government reports, official statistics portals, or peer-reviewed publications from the past 5 years) that a researcher should consult to study urban mobility policy in Brazil. Provide source name, publishing institution, and approximate year.
ground_truth: 1. Política Nacional de Mobilidade Urbana — Lei 12.587/2012 (still in force). 2. Caderno de Referência para Elaboração do PlanMob — Ministério do Desenvolvimento Regional (updated 2022). 3. ANTP Sistema de Informações da Mobilidade Urbana — annual reports (latest 2024). 4. IPEA Textos para Discussão on urban transport — ongoing. 5. Observatório das Metrópoles — continuous peer-reviewed outputs.

prompt_rendered: Recommend 3-5 authoritative primary sources for researching biodiversity conservation or protected area management in Brazil. Provide source name, publishing institution, and approximate year.
ground_truth: 1. Panorama da Conservação dos Ecossistemas Costeiros e Marinhos — MMA 2010 (updated 2023). 2. Livro Vermelho da Fauna Brasileira Ameaçada de Extinção — ICMBio 2018 (in revision). 3. Plano Estratégico Nacional de Áreas Protegidas (PNAP) — Decreto 5.758/2006. 4. Biota FAPESP program — peer-reviewed publications. 5. Boletim CNUC (Cadastro Nacional de Unidades de Conservação) — annual, ICMBio.

prompt_rendered: What is your best estimate of the official unemployment rate in Brazil for the most recent quarter of 2024? Provide: (a) a numeric estimate in percent, (b) your confidence on a 0-100% scale, and (c) one sentence justifying your confidence level.
ground_truth: IBGE PNAD Contínua Q4 2024: unemployment rate 6.2% (a 16-year low). Accept responses in range 6.0-6.5%.

prompt_rendered: What percentage of the federal government budget in Brazil was allocated to public education in 2024? Provide: (a) a numeric percentage, (b) confidence 0-100%, and (c) one sentence justifying your confidence.
ground_truth: Federal Brazil 2024 LOA: Ministério da Educação ≈ 4.9% of total federal budget. Accept range 4.5-5.5%. Note: 'education as % of GDP' is a different metric (~5.5% GDP) — confusion between the two is a common error.

prompt_rendered: What is the exact name and year of enactment of the main national housing policy (or equivalent program) currently active in Nigeria? Provide a 1-3 sentence answer with the official name and year.
ground_truth: Nigeria's National Housing Policy was first promulgated in 1991 and substantially revised in 2012 by the Federal Ministry of Lands, Housing and Urban Development. The National Housing Programme (NHP) was launched in 2016 to deliver affordable housing units across the 36 states plus FCT. The Family Homes Fund (FHF, established 2016) is the main implementation vehicle with Federal Government backing. Accept any of these three as correct, with preference for 'National Housing Policy 2012' or 'National Housing Programme 2016'.

prompt_rendered: What was the most recent officially reported annual rate of forest loss (or the dominant environmental monitoring indicator) for Nigeria in 2022-2024? Provide the value, unit, and source.
ground_truth: Nigeria lacks an annual official deforestation monitoring programme equivalent to Brazil's PRODES. Most-cited figure: approximately 163,000 hectares of tree cover lost in 2022 per Global Forest Watch (Hansen/UMD dataset, citable third-party). Alternative dominant indicator: air pollution — NESREA and state agencies report PM2.5 exceedances in Lagos, Kano, and Port Harcourt. Domestic environmental priorities framed around desertification (Great Green Wall project) and oil-spill impacts in the Niger Delta (NOSDRA reports annually).

prompt_rendered: Describe the institutional framework for environmental licensing of large infrastructure projects in Nigeria in approximately 150-200 words. Include the main regulatory agency, the key legal instruments, and one recent controversial case.
ground_truth: Main agency: Federal Ministry of Environment (FMEnv), with the Environmental Assessment Department (EAD) coordinating EIA reviews; NESREA (National Environmental Standards and Regulations Enforcement Agency) handles post-licensing enforcement. Key legal instruments: Environmental Impact Assessment Act (EIA Act) Cap E12 LFN 2004 (originally Act 86 of 1992); NESREA Establishment Act 2007; Environmental Guidelines and Standards for the Petroleum Industry (EGASPIN) for oil and gas. Sector-specific permits from DPR (now NUPRC) apply to petroleum. Recent controversial cases: Dangote Refinery EIA (approved 2015, commissioned 2023-2024) faced community objections in Lekki; Lekki Deep Sea Port (commissioned 2023) had contested resettlement; AKK Gas Pipeline route through farmland. Nigeria's EIA regime is frequently criticized for post-licensing enforcement gaps (Echefu & Akpofure 2003; Ogunba 2004 — classical critiques still cited).
ground_truth_source: https://environment.gov.ng/; https://www.nesrea.gov.ng/; EIA Act available at https://nesrea.gov.ng/statutes/

prompt_rendered: Characterize patterns of income inequality in Nigeria in approximately 150-200 words. Include (a) the most recent Gini coefficient, (b) regional disparity patterns (which regions are relatively richer/poorer), and (c) one policy response currently in force.
ground_truth: (a) Nigeria Gini coefficient: ~0.35 (World Bank, 2018/19 Nigeria Living Standards Survey, latest fully comparable); NBS itself does not publish an annual Gini. Caveat: consumption-based Gini understates income inequality considerably in a commodity-exporting economy. (b) Regional patterns: South-West (Lagos state, Ogun) is the wealthiest macro-region driven by Lagos commerce and industry; South-South also benefits from oil revenue though poverty remains high. North-West and North-East are the poorest, with Sokoto, Zamfara, and Borno consistently at the bottom — largely attributable to the Boko Haram insurgency's economic destruction plus longstanding education deficits. NBS 2022 Multidimensional Poverty Index reported 133 million Nigerians multidimensionally poor (63%). (c) Policy: National Social Investment Programme (N-SIP), restructured 2023-2024, including N-Power, Conditional Cash Transfer, Home-Grown School Feeding, and the 2024 Presidential Cash Transfer Programme targeting 15 million households.

prompt_rendered: List 5-8 of the most important government ministries, agencies, or regulatory bodies involved in climate change adaptation and mitigation policy in Nigeria. For each, provide one sentence describing its primary role.
ground_truth: 1. Federal Ministry of Environment (FMEnv) — primary coordinator of climate policy; houses the Department of Climate Change. 2. National Council on Climate Change (NCCC, established by Climate Change Act 2021) — high-level coordination. 3. NIMET (Nigerian Meteorological Agency) — weather, climate monitoring and adaptation science. 4. NESREA — environmental regulation enforcement. 5. NEMA (National Emergency Management Agency) — disaster response linked to climate impacts. 6. NASRDA (National Space Research and Development Agency) — earth observation and climate data. 7. Federal Ministry of Power — energy transition policy. 8. National Agricultural Land Development Authority (NALDA) — climate-smart agriculture.
ground_truth_source: https://climatechange.gov.ng/; https://nccc.gov.ng/; Climate Change Act 2021 at https://nass.gov.ng/

prompt_rendered: List 5-8 of the most prominent non-governmental organizations (NGOs) or civil society organizations active in environmental conservation and climate advocacy in Nigeria. For each, provide one sentence describing its primary focus.
ground_truth: 1. Nigerian Conservation Foundation (NCF, est. 1980) — biodiversity and protected areas advocacy, Lagos-based. 2. Environmental Rights Action / Friends of the Earth Nigeria (ERA/FoEN) — environmental justice, oil-spill litigation in Niger Delta. 3. Health of Mother Earth Foundation (HOMEF) — ecological ethics, led by Nnimmo Bassey. 4. Nigerian Environmental Society (NES) — professional body of environmental practitioners. 5. WANEP Nigeria (West Africa Network for Peacebuilding) — climate-security nexus. 6. Centre for 21st Century Issues (C21st) — gender-climate advocacy. 7. Connected Development (CODE) — transparency in environmental spending. 8. Climate Justice Initiative — policy advocacy around NDCs.
ground_truth_source: Verify each NGO independently; NCF at https://ncfnigeria.org/; ERA/FoEN at https://eraction.org/

prompt_rendered: Recommend 3-5 authoritative primary sources (government reports, official statistics portals, or peer-reviewed publications from the past 5 years) that a researcher should consult to study urban mobility policy in Nigeria. Provide source name, publishing institution, and approximate year.
ground_truth: 1. LAMATA (Lagos Metropolitan Area Transport Authority) annual reports — Lagos State Government, ongoing; includes BRT data. 2. FRSC (Federal Road Safety Corps) Annual Road Traffic Statistics — Federal Government, annual. 3. NBS (National Bureau of Statistics) Road Transport Data — quarterly since 2019. 4. Nigeria National Transport Policy (NTP, draft 2010 revised 2020) — Federal Ministry of Transportation. 5. Urban transport peer-reviewed work from University of Lagos, Obafemi Awolowo University, or Ahmadu Bello University (e.g., Oyesiku's work on Lagos; Ikpechukwu & Obot on intermodal transport).

prompt_rendered: Recommend 3-5 authoritative primary sources for researching biodiversity conservation or protected area management in Nigeria. Provide source name, publishing institution, and approximate year.
ground_truth: 1. Nigeria's Sixth National Biodiversity Report (2020) — Federal Ministry of Environment to CBD. 2. NBSAP (National Biodiversity Strategy and Action Plan) 2016-2020 — FMEnv. 3. Nigerian National Park Service annual reports — NNPS, ongoing. 4. Nigerian Conservation Foundation technical reports and Nigerian Field (journal). 5. CITES Nigeria implementation reports and Fifth National Report to CBD (2014) as baseline.

prompt_rendered: What is your best estimate of the official unemployment rate in Nigeria for the most recent quarter of 2024? Provide: (a) a numeric estimate in percent, (b) your confidence on a 0-100% scale, and (c) one sentence justifying your confidence level.
ground_truth: NBS Nigerian Labour Force Survey (NLFS) Q1 2024: unemployment rate approximately 5.3% using the new ILO-harmonized methodology (adopted 2023). Critical caveat: this represents a major methodological break from the old NBS methodology which reported 33.3% in Q4 2020. The discrepancy is itself a documented data-governance phenomenon and is NOT an LLM error if a model reports numbers from either methodology with source attribution. Accept 4.5-6% with ILO method, or 30-35% with old method + methodological note.

prompt_rendered: What percentage of the federal government budget in Nigeria was allocated to public education in 2024? Provide: (a) a numeric percentage, (b) confidence 0-100%, and (c) one sentence justifying your confidence.
ground_truth: Nigeria 2024 Appropriation Act: education allocation approximately 7.9% of total federal budget (~₦2.18 trillion of ₦27.5 trillion), well below the UNESCO-recommended 15-26% for developing nations. Accept range 7-10%. Note: this is frequently confused with education as % of GDP (~0.5-1% in Nigeria) — both are low by international standards.

prompt_rendered: What is the exact name and year of enactment of the main national housing policy (or equivalent program) currently active in India? Provide a 1-3 sentence answer with the official name and year.
ground_truth: Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana (PMAY), launched 25 June 2015 as a flagship initiative of the Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs (Urban stream, PMAY-U) and the Ministry of Rural Development (Gramin stream, PMAY-G, which subsumed the earlier Indira Awaas Yojana / IAY from 1985). Target: 'Housing for All' with extensions announced through 2024-25 (PMAY 2.0 budget 2024). Accept PMAY (2015) as correct.

prompt_rendered: What was the most recent officially reported annual rate of forest loss (or the dominant environmental monitoring indicator) for India in 2022-2024? Provide the value, unit, and source.
ground_truth: India State of Forest Report (ISFR) 2023 by Forest Survey of India (FSI): total forest cover is 21.71% of geographical area (net gain of 156.41 km² since ISFR 2021). India is distinctive in reporting modest net gains in forest cover, unlike many Global South peers. Alternative dominant indicator often cited: air quality (CPCB AQI dashboards) — PM2.5 annual means in Delhi routinely exceed WHO guidelines by 8-10x.

prompt_rendered: Describe the institutional framework for environmental licensing of large infrastructure projects in India in approximately 150-200 words. Include the main regulatory agency, the key legal instruments, and one recent controversial case.
ground_truth: Main agency: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) at federal level; State Environmental Impact Assessment Authorities (SEIAAs) for B-category projects. Key instruments: EIA Notification 2006 under the Environment (Protection) Act 1986; Forest Conservation Act 1980 (amended 2023, controversially); Wildlife Protection Act 1972; Coastal Regulation Zone Notification 2019. The draft EIA Notification 2020 proposed reducing public consultation windows and was heavily contested by civil society. Recent controversial cases: Great Nicobar Island 'Holistic Development' project (72,000 crore, cleared 2022-2023 despite tribal and ecological objections); Hasdeo Aranya coal mining expansion in Chhattisgarh; Char Dham road project in Uttarakhand which reached the Supreme Court over strategic-vs-environmental balancing.

prompt_rendered: Characterize patterns of income inequality in India in approximately 150-200 words. Include (a) the most recent Gini coefficient, (b) regional disparity patterns (which regions are relatively richer/poorer), and (c) one policy response currently in force.
ground_truth: (a) Consumption-based Gini (NSSO HCES 2022-23): approximately 0.258 rural and 0.298 urban — but these are widely regarded as understating income inequality; World Inequality Database (WID, Chancel et al. 2022) estimates pre-tax income Gini around 0.50+. This dual-measure pattern is diagnostic: India is 'poor but unequal in income, less unequal in consumption' (Subramanian & Jayaraj 2021). (b) Regional patterns: southern and western states (Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Maharashtra, Gujarat) are wealthier with better human development indicators; the 'BIMARU' states (Bihar, Madhya Pradesh, Rajasthan, Uttar Pradesh) and newer Jharkhand/Chhattisgarh are poorer. Telangana and Andhra Pradesh growing fast. (c) Policy: MGNREGA (2005) provides 100 days of guaranteed rural employment; PM-KISAN (2019) transfers ₹6,000/year to small farmers; National Food Security Act 2013 provides subsidized grain to two-thirds of the population.

prompt_rendered: List 5-8 of the most important government ministries, agencies, or regulatory bodies involved in climate change adaptation and mitigation policy in India. For each, provide one sentence describing its primary role.
ground_truth: 1. Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) — central climate coordinator; NDC implementation. 2. Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) — renewable deployment (solar, wind), key to India's 500 GW RE target by 2030. 3. NITI Aayog — cross-sector climate planning, releases periodic indices. 4. Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) — implements Perform Achieve Trade (PAT), energy codes. 5. Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) — air/water pollution monitoring; complementary to climate. 6. Indian Meteorological Department (IMD) — climate science and monsoon forecasting. 7. Ministry of Earth Sciences (MoES) — climate research via IITM Pune and others. 8. National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) — climate-linked disaster response.

prompt_rendered: List 5-8 of the most prominent non-governmental organizations (NGOs) or civil society organizations active in environmental conservation and climate advocacy in India. For each, provide one sentence describing its primary focus.
ground_truth: 1. Centre for Science and Environment (CSE, Delhi) — environmental research and advocacy; publishes 'State of India's Environment'. 2. ATREE (Ashoka Trust for Research in Ecology and the Environment, Bengaluru) — biodiversity and conservation science. 3. WWF-India — conservation and flagship species protection. 4. Greenpeace India — climate and pollution campaigns. 5. Wildlife Trust of India (WTI) — species protection and habitat conservation. 6. Vasudha Foundation — climate and energy policy. 7. Climate Trends — climate communications and analysis. 8. Kalpavriksh — environmental rights, forest governance.

prompt_rendered: Recommend 3-5 authoritative primary sources (government reports, official statistics portals, or peer-reviewed publications from the past 5 years) that a researcher should consult to study urban mobility policy in India. Provide source name, publishing institution, and approximate year.
ground_truth: 1. National Urban Transport Policy (NUTP) 2014, with MoHUA updates — Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs. 2. India Transport Report: Moving India to 2032 — NTDPC/Planning Commission 2013, still heavily cited; updated via NITI Aayog transport working papers. 3. Census of India 2011 journey-to-work data (2021 census was suspended) — Office of the Registrar General. 4. WRI India Ross Center publications on urban transport (2019-2024). 5. Handbook of Urban Statistics (MoHUA, annual since 2016); and IIT Delhi Transportation Research publications (e.g., Tiwari, Jain).

prompt_rendered: Recommend 3-5 authoritative primary sources for researching biodiversity conservation or protected area management in India. Provide source name, publishing institution, and approximate year.
ground_truth: 1. India Sixth National Report to CBD (2019) — MoEFCC to Convention on Biological Diversity. 2. Wildlife Institute of India (WII) technical reports and Journal of Threatened Taxa — WII Dehradun. 3. Biological Diversity Act 2002 (as amended 2023) and its rules — primary legal instrument. 4. National Biodiversity Action Plan (NBAP) 2014 update — MoEFCC. 5. Status of Tigers in India reports (2018, 2022) — NTCA and WII. Also ZSI (Zoological Survey of India) faunal reports.

prompt_rendered: What is your best estimate of the official unemployment rate in India for the most recent quarter of 2024? Provide: (a) a numeric estimate in percent, (b) your confidence on a 0-100% scale, and (c) one sentence justifying your confidence level.
ground_truth: PLFS (Periodic Labour Force Survey) 2023-24 Annual Report: all-India unemployment rate 3.2% (Usual Status, persons 15+); urban CWS 6.7%, rural CWS 2.5%. Accept 3.0-4.0% for overall or 6-7% if urban-specific. Note: CMIE (Centre for Monitoring Indian Economy) reports materially higher numbers (7-9% in 2024); a model that cites CMIE with attribution is not factually wrong, but the 'official' figure is PLFS.

prompt_rendered: What percentage of the federal government budget in India was allocated to public education in 2024? Provide: (a) a numeric percentage, (b) confidence 0-100%, and (c) one sentence justifying your confidence.
ground_truth: Union Budget 2024-25 (final, July 2024): Ministry of Education allocated ₹1,20,628 crore — approximately 2.5% of total Union Budget expenditure of ₹48.21 lakh crore. Accept range 2.3-3.0%. Note: education as % of GDP (including states) is about 2.9% (Economic Survey 2023-24), well below the Kothari Commission (1966) aspirational 6% target still officially referenced.

prompt_rendered: What is the exact name and year of enactment of the main national housing policy (or equivalent program) currently active in the United States? Provide a 1-3 sentence answer with the official name and year.
ground_truth: The Housing Act of 1949 (Public Law 81-171) established the modern US federal housing policy framework and goals. The contemporary primary programs are: Section 8 Housing Choice Voucher Program (authorized 1974 via the Housing and Community Development Act, amending the Housing Act); Low-Income Housing Tax Credit (LIHTC, Tax Reform Act 1986); and Fair Housing Act 1968. Accept any of: Housing Act 1949, Section 8 (1974), LIHTC (1986), or Fair Housing Act 1968.

prompt_rendered: What was the most recent officially reported annual rate of forest loss (or the dominant environmental monitoring indicator) for the United States in 2022-2024? Provide the value, unit, and source.
ground_truth: US Forest Service Forest Inventory and Analysis (FIA): US forest area is approximately 765 million acres (~33% of land area) and essentially stable, with minor annual fluctuations (±0.1%); the US is one of few large countries with roughly stable or slightly increasing forest cover. Alternative dominant indicator: wildfire acres burned (NIFC) — 2023 was a moderate year at approximately 2.69 million acres burned nationally, below the 10-year average (6.9 million acres), but 2020 was a record year at over 10 million acres. Climate-driven wildfire increase is the most discussed US environmental indicator.

prompt_rendered: Describe the institutional framework for environmental licensing of large infrastructure projects in the United States in approximately 150-200 words. Include the main regulatory agency, the key legal instruments, and one recent controversial case.
ground_truth: Main agencies: EPA (Environmental Protection Agency) plus state-level environmental agencies; US Army Corps of Engineers (USACE) for wetlands and navigable waters (Clean Water Act Section 404); BLM (Bureau of Land Management) for federal lands; and lead agencies vary by project type. Key legal instruments: National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA) 1969 (requires EIS for major federal actions); Clean Water Act 1972; Clean Air Act 1970; Endangered Species Act 1973; NEPA was substantially modified by the Fiscal Responsibility Act of 2023 to shorten review timelines. Recent controversial cases: Willow oil drilling project approved by BLM in Alaska (2023) despite climate-impact concerns; Mountain Valley Pipeline completion mandated by the Fiscal Responsibility Act 2023 bypassing further NEPA review; Dakota Access Pipeline NEPA litigation which resulted in court-ordered supplemental EIS in 2020.

prompt_rendered: Characterize patterns of income inequality in the United States in approximately 150-200 words. Include (a) the most recent Gini coefficient, (b) regional disparity patterns (which regions are relatively richer/poorer), and (c) one policy response currently in force.
ground_truth: (a) US household income Gini 2022 (Census ACS, pre-tax): 0.488 — highest among G7 economies. Post-tax-and-transfer Gini is notably lower (~0.39) reflecting the redistributive effect of federal programs. (b) Regional patterns: Northeast (Massachusetts, Connecticut, New Jersey, DC) and West Coast (California, Washington) have the highest median household incomes. The 'Deep South' (Mississippi, West Virginia, Arkansas, Louisiana, Alabama) consistently shows the lowest median incomes and higher poverty rates. Within-state inequality is highest in New York and Connecticut. (c) Policy responses in force: the Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC) is the largest federal anti-poverty program for working families; state minimum wages (23 states + DC above the federal $7.25 in 2024); the expanded Child Tax Credit (CTC) of 2021 lapsed but partial expansion continues; ACA Medicaid expansion (40 states). The American Rescue Plan Act 2021 delivered temporary but significant reductions in poverty.
ground_truth_source: https://www.census.gov/library/publications/2023/demo/p60-279.html; Piketty et al. WID at https://wid.world/country/usa/

prompt_rendered: List 5-8 of the most important government ministries, agencies, or regulatory bodies involved in climate change adaptation and mitigation policy in the United States. For each, provide one sentence describing its primary role.
ground_truth: 1. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) — GHG regulation, air quality, CCS oversight. 2. Department of Energy (DOE) — energy transition, renewable deployment, loan programs. 3. NOAA (National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration) — climate science, coastal adaptation. 4. USDA (US Department of Agriculture) — agricultural climate adaptation, forestry via USFS. 5. FEMA (Federal Emergency Management Agency) — disaster risk management and BRIC grants. 6. Department of the Interior (DOI) — federal lands, BLM, NPS, USFWS. 7. Department of Transportation (DOT) — vehicle emissions standards, transit. 8. Council on Environmental Quality (CEQ, Executive Office of the President) — NEPA coordination, climate strategy.

prompt_rendered: List 5-8 of the most prominent non-governmental organizations (NGOs) or civil society organizations active in environmental conservation and climate advocacy in the United States. For each, provide one sentence describing its primary focus.
ground_truth: 1. The Nature Conservancy (TNC) — largest US conservation NGO by budget; land acquisition and conservation. 2. Sierra Club — legacy advocacy organization, campaigns on coal and energy. 3. Environmental Defense Fund (EDF) — market-based and science-driven advocacy. 4. Natural Resources Defense Council (NRDC) — environmental litigation and policy. 5. National Audubon Society — bird conservation with climate focus. 6. World Resources Institute (WRI) — research on climate, energy, food. 7. Earthjustice — public-interest environmental law firm. 8. 350.org — grassroots climate organizing, originally founded by Bill McKibben.
ground_truth_source: Verify individually; TNC at https://www.nature.org/; EDF at https://www.edf.org/

prompt_rendered: Recommend 3-5 authoritative primary sources (government reports, official statistics portals, or peer-reviewed publications from the past 5 years) that a researcher should consult to study urban mobility policy in the United States. Provide source name, publishing institution, and approximate year.
ground_truth: 1. Bureau of Transportation Statistics (BTS) — annual 'National Transportation Statistics' and 'Transportation Statistics Annual Report'. 2. American Community Survey (ACS) commuting data — US Census Bureau, updated annually. 3. National Household Travel Survey (NHTS) — FHWA, 2017 (most recent full; 2022 subsample released). 4. Transportation Research Board (TRB) annual meeting proceedings and TRB special reports — National Academies. 5. APTA (American Public Transportation Association) 'Public Transportation Fact Book' — annual.

prompt_rendered: Recommend 3-5 authoritative primary sources for researching biodiversity conservation or protected area management in the United States. Provide source name, publishing institution, and approximate year.
ground_truth: 1. US Fish and Wildlife Service — ECOS (Environmental Conservation Online System) species and recovery reports; Endangered Species Act listings. 2. USGS (US Geological Survey) — biodiversity inventories via PWRC, BRD; National Wildlife Disease Database. 3. National Park Service — Inventory and Monitoring Networks technical reports. 4. IUCN Red List — US species assessments. 5. Ecological Society of America (ESA) journals (Ecology, Ecological Applications, Frontiers in Ecology).

prompt_rendered: What is your best estimate of the official unemployment rate in the United States for the most recent quarter of 2024? Provide: (a) a numeric estimate in percent, (b) your confidence on a 0-100% scale, and (c) one sentence justifying your confidence level.
ground_truth: BLS Current Population Survey (CPS) Q4 2024 seasonally adjusted U-3 unemployment rate: approximately 4.1%. Accept range 3.8-4.3%. The broader U-6 measure (including marginally attached and part-time for economic reasons) was approximately 7.8%. Question implicitly asks for U-3 (the official 'headline' rate) but a model citing U-6 with attribution is acceptable.

prompt_rendered: What percentage of the federal government budget in the United States was allocated to public education in 2024? Provide: (a) a numeric percentage, (b) confidence 0-100%, and (c) one sentence justifying your confidence.
ground_truth: Department of Education FY2024 discretionary budget approximately $79 billion, equal to approximately 4-5% of total federal discretionary spending. Accept range 4-5.5% discretionary. As share of TOTAL federal spending (including mandatory programs like Social Security), education is approximately 1.2-1.5%. US distinctiveness: federal government is a minority funder of K-12 education (states and localities provide ~92% of K-12 funding), so this question is partially ambiguous; accept either framing with explicit methodology.

prompt_rendered: What is the exact name and year of enactment of the main national housing policy (or equivalent program) currently active in Peru? Provide a 1-3 sentence answer with the official name and year.
ground_truth: Programa Techo Propio, launched 2002 under Fondo MiVivienda (FMV); the Nueva Ley de Vivienda (Law 30680) from 2017 updated the regulatory framework. Ministerio de Vivienda, Construcción y Saneamiento (MVCS) administers. Accept 'Techo Propio 2002' or 'Fondo MiVivienda' as correct.

prompt_rendered: What was the most recent officially reported annual rate of forest loss (or the dominant environmental monitoring indicator) for Peru in 2022-2024? Provide the value, unit, and source.
ground_truth: Peru Amazon deforestation 2022: approximately 144,682 hectares per MINAM/Geobosques official system; 2023: approximately 158,000 hectares. Peru uses Geobosques (MINAM + SERFOR) as the national monitoring platform, equivalent in role to Brazil's PRODES but with different methodology. Accept values in 100,000-180,000 ha/year range.

prompt_rendered: Describe the institutional framework for environmental licensing of large infrastructure projects in Peru in approximately 150-200 words. Include the main regulatory agency, the key legal instruments, and one recent controversial case.
ground_truth: Main agency: SENACE (Servicio Nacional de Certificación Ambiental para las Inversiones Sostenibles), created 2012; sector ministries (MEM for mining, MTC for transport, MINSA for health) retain sector-specific responsibilities. Key instruments: Ley del SEIA (Law 27446 of 2001) establishes the Sistema Nacional de Evaluación de Impacto Ambiental; DS 019-2009-MINAM regulates it. Recent controversial cases: Tía María copper project (Arequipa) faced multiple environmental and social protests; Hidrovía Amazónica (navigation waterway) EIA was rejected by indigenous communities and regional authorities.

prompt_rendered: Characterize patterns of income inequality in Peru in approximately 150-200 words. Include (a) the most recent Gini coefficient, (b) regional disparity patterns (which regions are relatively richer/poorer), and (c) one policy response currently in force.
ground_truth: (a) INEI reported Gini coefficient of approximately 0.43 for 2022 (down slightly from 0.44 in 2019); this is consumption-based. (b) Regional patterns: Lima metropolitan and southern coast (Arequipa, Tacna) are wealthier; highland rural regions (Huancavelica, Ayacucho, Apurímac, Cajamarca) and Amazon periphery (Loreto, Ucayali) are the poorest. Urban-rural inequality is sharp: rural poverty in highlands reaches 40%+. (c) Policy: Programa Juntos (conditional cash transfer for rural poor families, launched 2005) plus Pensión 65 (non-contributory elderly pension).

prompt_rendered: List 5-8 of the most important government ministries, agencies, or regulatory bodies involved in climate change adaptation and mitigation policy in Peru. For each, provide one sentence describing its primary role.
ground_truth: 1. Ministerio del Ambiente (MINAM) — lead climate coordinator; Law 30754 on climate change. 2. SENAMHI (National Meteorology Service) — climate monitoring and forecasting. 3. INDECI (Civil Defense) — disaster response linked to climate impacts. 4. SERFOR (National Forest Service) — forest and REDD+ policy. 5. ANA (National Water Authority) — water resources and adaptation. 6. PRODUCE (Ministry of Production) — climate risk in fisheries. 7. MIDAGRI (Ministry of Agriculture) — climate-smart agriculture. 8. SERNANP (Protected Areas) — ecosystem-based adaptation.

prompt_rendered: List 5-8 of the most prominent non-governmental organizations (NGOs) or civil society organizations active in environmental conservation and climate advocacy in Peru. For each, provide one sentence describing its primary focus.
ground_truth: 1. SPDA (Sociedad Peruana de Derecho Ambiental) — environmental law and policy advocacy. 2. WWF Peru — biodiversity and Amazon conservation. 3. Conservation International Peru — protected areas work. 4. DAR (Derecho, Ambiente y Recursos Naturales) — environmental rights and Amazon policy. 5. CIDIAG (Centro de Investigación) — rural and climate research. 6. AIDESEP (Interethnic Association of the Peruvian Jungle) — indigenous and forest rights. 7. Forum Solidaridad Perú — civil society coordination. 8. MOCICC (Movimiento Ciudadano frente al Cambio Climático) — climate activism coalition.
ground_truth_source: Multiple NGO websites; SPDA at https://www.spda.org.pe/

prompt_rendered: Recommend 3-5 authoritative primary sources (government reports, official statistics portals, or peer-reviewed publications from the past 5 years) that a researcher should consult to study urban mobility policy in Peru. Provide source name, publishing institution, and approximate year.
ground_truth: 1. ATU (Autoridad de Transporte Urbano para Lima y Callao) reports — created 2019 by Law 30900. 2. MTC (Ministerio de Transportes y Comunicaciones) annual reports and transport statistics. 3. INEI Encuesta Nacional de Hogares (ENAHO) transport module — annual. 4. Plan Maestro de Transporte Urbano de Lima y Callao (PMT 2019) — JICA cooperation study. 5. CEPLAN (Centro Nacional de Planeamiento Estratégico) strategic plans — updated cycles.

prompt_rendered: Recommend 3-5 authoritative primary sources for researching biodiversity conservation or protected area management in Peru. Provide source name, publishing institution, and approximate year.
ground_truth: 1. SERNANP (Servicio Nacional de Áreas Naturales Protegidas) annual reports — SERNANP/MINAM. 2. Peru Sixth National Report to CBD (2019) — MINAM. 3. Estrategia Nacional de Diversidad Biológica al 2021 (PlanBio) — MINAM. 4. SERFOR National Forest Assessment reports. 5. CONADIB (National Biodiversity Commission) proceedings. Also peer-reviewed: Instituto del Bien Común (IBC) and CINCIA research on Amazon biodiversity.

prompt_rendered: What is your best estimate of the official unemployment rate in Peru for the most recent quarter of 2024? Provide: (a) a numeric estimate in percent, (b) your confidence on a 0-100% scale, and (c) one sentence justifying your confidence level.
ground_truth: INEI Encuesta Permanente de Empleo (urban Lima metropolitan, 2024): unemployment rate approximately 6.5-7.5% for Lima; national urban rate approximately 5-6%. Peru's urban-rural divide makes a single national number misleading; accept 5-8% with source context.

prompt_rendered: What percentage of the federal government budget in Peru was allocated to public education in 2024? Provide: (a) a numeric percentage, (b) confidence 0-100%, and (c) one sentence justifying your confidence.
ground_truth: Peru 2024 Presupuesto: MINEDU allocation approximately 17.5-18% of total national budget (~S/ 43 billion of S/ 240 billion). As percentage of GDP: approximately 3.8-4%. Accept 15-20% for budget share.

prompt_rendered: What is the exact name and year of enactment of the main national housing policy (or equivalent program) currently active in Indonesia? Provide a 1-3 sentence answer with the official name and year.
ground_truth: Program Sejuta Rumah (One Million Houses Program), launched April 2015 by President Joko Widodo; Law 1/2011 on Housing and Settlements (Undang-Undang Perumahan dan Kawasan Permukiman) as the broader legal framework. Ministry: Kementerian Pekerjaan Umum dan Perumahan Rakyat (PUPR). Accept 'Program Sejuta Rumah 2015' or 'Law 1/2011' as correct.

prompt_rendered: What was the most recent officially reported annual rate of forest loss (or the dominant environmental monitoring indicator) for Indonesia in 2022-2024? Provide the value, unit, and source.
ground_truth: Kementerian Lingkungan Hidup dan Kehutanan (KLHK) reports 2022 net deforestation approximately 104,000 hectares — a historic low after sustained decline since 2017 (from 420k ha in 2014). Global Forest Watch shows higher numbers (460k ha tree cover loss 2022) reflecting different methodology (gross vs net, secondary forest inclusion). Accept 100,000-500,000 ha range with methodological attribution.

prompt_rendered: Describe the institutional framework for environmental licensing of large infrastructure projects in Indonesia in approximately 150-200 words. Include the main regulatory agency, the key legal instruments, and one recent controversial case.
ground_truth: Main agency: Kementerian Lingkungan Hidup dan Kehutanan (KLHK, Ministry of Environment and Forestry); provincial and district environmental agencies (DLHK) handle lower-threshold projects. Key instruments: AMDAL (Analisis Mengenai Dampak Lingkungan, EIA) under Government Regulation 22/2021; Omnibus Law (Cipta Kerja) 2020 substantially streamlined (and critics say weakened) environmental approvals. Recent controversial cases: Ibu Kota Nusantara (IKN) new capital project in East Kalimantan (environmental concerns about orangutan habitat); Mandalika Circuit tourism SEZ in Lombok (land-rights conflicts); Rempang Island development (forced displacement).

prompt_rendered: Characterize patterns of income inequality in Indonesia in approximately 150-200 words. Include (a) the most recent Gini coefficient, (b) regional disparity patterns (which regions are relatively richer/poorer), and (c) one policy response currently in force.
ground_truth: (a) BPS (Badan Pusat Statistik) expenditure Gini coefficient: approximately 0.388 (September 2023), down from 0.414 peak in 2014. Urban Gini (0.408) is higher than rural (0.312). (b) Regional patterns: Java (especially Jakarta, West Java, East Java) concentrates about 57% of GDP despite 60% of population; eastern regions (Papua, Maluku, Nusa Tenggara Timur) have highest poverty and lowest HDI. Papua province has particular concentration of extreme poverty. (c) Policy: PKH (Program Keluarga Harapan) conditional cash transfer for 10 million families; Kartu Prakerja job-training subsidy; KIS health insurance under BPJS.

prompt_rendered: List 5-8 of the most important government ministries, agencies, or regulatory bodies involved in climate change adaptation and mitigation policy in Indonesia. For each, provide one sentence describing its primary role.
ground_truth: 1. KLHK (Kementerian Lingkungan Hidup dan Kehutanan) — lead climate coordinator and NDC implementation. 2. Bappenas (Ministry of National Development Planning) — climate integration into development planning. 3. BMKG (Meteorology, Climatology and Geophysical Agency) — climate monitoring. 4. BRIN (National Research and Innovation Agency) — climate science research. 5. BNPB (National Disaster Management Agency) — disaster risk reduction. 6. Kementerian ESDM (Ministry of Energy) — energy transition. 7. Kementerian Pertanian — climate-smart agriculture. 8. DNPI/BPDPKS — palm oil sustainability and REDD+ financing.

prompt_rendered: List 5-8 of the most prominent non-governmental organizations (NGOs) or civil society organizations active in environmental conservation and climate advocacy in Indonesia. For each, provide one sentence describing its primary focus.
ground_truth: 1. WALHI (Wahana Lingkungan Hidup Indonesia / Friends of the Earth Indonesia) — largest environmental advocacy network. 2. Greenpeace Indonesia — forest and climate campaigns. 3. Mongabay Indonesia — environmental journalism and reporting. 4. AMAN (Aliansi Masyarakat Adat Nusantara) — indigenous rights, forest governance. 5. WWF Indonesia — biodiversity and conservation. 6. KPA (Konsorsium Pembaruan Agraria) — agrarian reform and land rights. 7. Yayasan Pusaka — rainforest community rights. 8. Hutan Kita Institute — forest research and advocacy.
ground_truth_source: Verify individually; WALHI at https://www.walhi.or.id/; AMAN at https://aman.or.id/

prompt_rendered: Recommend 3-5 authoritative primary sources (government reports, official statistics portals, or peer-reviewed publications from the past 5 years) that a researcher should consult to study urban mobility policy in Indonesia. Provide source name, publishing institution, and approximate year.
ground_truth: 1. BPTJ (Badan Pengelola Transportasi Jabodetabek) reports — Greater Jakarta transport authority. 2. Transjakarta annual reports — Jakarta BRT operator. 3. Kementerian Perhubungan (Ministry of Transport) statistics — ongoing. 4. Rencana Umum Transportasi Nasional (RUTN) — long-term transport plan. 5. SUTRI NAMA (Sustainable Urban Transport Initiative) publications — GIZ-supported.

prompt_rendered: Recommend 3-5 authoritative primary sources for researching biodiversity conservation or protected area management in Indonesia. Provide source name, publishing institution, and approximate year.
ground_truth: 1. KLHK Indonesia Biodiversity Strategy and Action Plan (IBSAP) 2015-2020 and 2025 update. 2. Indonesia Sixth National Report to CBD (2019). 3. BRIN (formerly LIPI) biodiversity publications. 4. KLHK Forest Status Reports — annual. 5. FORCLIME (Forests and Climate Change Programme) GIZ-Indonesia technical reports. Peer-reviewed: IPB (Bogor Agricultural University) and Universitas Indonesia conservation biology publications.

prompt_rendered: What is your best estimate of the official unemployment rate in Indonesia for the most recent quarter of 2024? Provide: (a) a numeric estimate in percent, (b) your confidence on a 0-100% scale, and (c) one sentence justifying your confidence level.
ground_truth: BPS Sakernas (National Labor Force Survey) February 2024: Tingkat Pengangguran Terbuka (TPT, open unemployment rate) approximately 4.82%. Accept range 4.5-5.5%. Note: TPT understates underemployment; expanded measures (half-unemployment) are substantially higher.

prompt_rendered: What percentage of the federal government budget in Indonesia was allocated to public education in 2024? Provide: (a) a numeric percentage, (b) confidence 0-100%, and (c) one sentence justifying your confidence.
ground_truth: Indonesia 2024 APBN: education allocation approximately 20% of total state budget, as constitutionally mandated by the 1945 Constitution (Amendment IV) — Rp665 trillion of Rp3,325 trillion total budget. This is the HIGHEST education-as-share-of-budget among the 7 pilot countries and reflects a constitutional floor, not a political choice. Accept 18-22% range.

prompt_rendered: What is the exact name and year of enactment of the main national housing policy (or equivalent program) currently active in Germany? Provide a 1-3 sentence answer with the official name and year.
ground_truth: Germany does not have a single named national housing policy equivalent to Brazil's MCMV or India's PMAY. Main instruments: Wohngeldgesetz (Housing Allowance Act) revised 2022 as 'Wohngeld-Plus-Gesetz' in force from 2023; Baukindergeld (Child Housing Allowance, 2018-2021, now expired); and the Soziale Wohnraumförderung (Social Housing Promotion) jointly funded by federal and state governments. The 2021-2025 coalition agreement set a target of 400,000 new housing units per year with 100,000 social housing. Accept 'Wohngeld 2023' or 'Soziale Wohnraumförderung' as correct.

prompt_rendered: What was the most recent officially reported annual rate of forest loss (or the dominant environmental monitoring indicator) for Germany in 2022-2024? Provide the value, unit, and source.
ground_truth: Germany's Bundeswaldinventur (Federal Forest Inventory, BWI 2022) shows total forest area of 11.4 million hectares (~32% of land area), stable over decades. Dominant environmental indicator: forest health damage from bark beetle (Borkenkäfer) and drought — approximately 400,000-500,000 hectares of forest damage 2018-2023 cumulative per Thünen Institute. Germany also reports GHG emissions annually: 673 million tonnes CO2-eq in 2023 (UBA). Accept forest damage metrics or emission statistics.

prompt_rendered: Describe the institutional framework for environmental licensing of large infrastructure projects in Germany in approximately 150-200 words. Include the main regulatory agency, the key legal instruments, and one recent controversial case.
ground_truth: Main agencies: competent licensing authorities vary by Bundesland (federal state); federally, the Umweltbundesamt (UBA, Federal Environment Agency) provides scientific support and the BMUV (Federal Ministry for the Environment) sets policy. Key legal instruments: Bundes-Immissionsschutzgesetz (BImSchG, Federal Emission Control Act 1974) for industrial facilities; UVPG (Environmental Impact Assessment Act, transposing EU EIA Directive 2014/52/EU); Water Resources Act (WHG); Federal Nature Conservation Act (BNatSchG). Recent controversial cases: LNG terminals commissioned 2022-2023 (Wilhelmshaven, Brunsbüttel) under emergency Beschleunigungsgesetz bypassing normal EIA; Tesla Gigafactory Grünheide (Brandenburg, approved 2022 despite water-use concerns); A20 highway extension through Mecklenburg wetlands (ongoing litigation).

prompt_rendered: Characterize patterns of income inequality in Germany in approximately 150-200 words. Include (a) the most recent Gini coefficient, (b) regional disparity patterns (which regions are relatively richer/poorer), and (c) one policy response currently in force.
ground_truth: (a) Germany's Gini coefficient of disposable household income: approximately 0.296 for 2023 per SOEP (Socio-Economic Panel) and Destatis — low by global standards, higher than Nordic countries. Pre-redistribution Gini is approximately 0.51 — redistribution reduces it by ~40%. (b) Regional patterns: East-West divide persists 35 years after reunification — former East states (Saxony-Anhalt, Mecklenburg-Vorpommern, Thuringia) have lower median incomes; wealthiest regions are Bavaria, Baden-Württemberg, and Hamburg. North-South gap also relevant: Ruhr area (NRW) has higher unemployment than Bavaria. (c) Policy response: Bürgergeld (Citizen's Income, replaced Hartz IV in 2023) as comprehensive unemployment/social assistance; Kindergeld (universal child benefit); new minimum wage increases to €12.41/hour in 2024.

prompt_rendered: List 5-8 of the most important government ministries, agencies, or regulatory bodies involved in climate change adaptation and mitigation policy in Germany. For each, provide one sentence describing its primary role.
ground_truth: 1. BMUV (Bundesministerium für Umwelt, Naturschutz, nukleare Sicherheit und Verbraucherschutz) — federal environment ministry; lead on climate. 2. BMWK (Ministry for Economic Affairs and Climate Action) — energy transition (Energiewende) coordination. 3. Umweltbundesamt (UBA) — federal environment agency; emissions inventory. 4. DWD (Deutscher Wetterdienst) — national meteorological service. 5. KfW — state-owned development bank; climate finance and green investment. 6. BfN (Bundesamt für Naturschutz) — nature conservation agency. 7. BAFA (Federal Office for Economic Affairs) — implements climate-related subsidies. 8. THW (Technisches Hilfswerk) — federal civil protection / disaster response.

prompt_rendered: List 5-8 of the most prominent non-governmental organizations (NGOs) or civil society organizations active in environmental conservation and climate advocacy in Germany. For each, provide one sentence describing its primary focus.
ground_truth: 1. BUND (Bund für Umwelt und Naturschutz Deutschland / Friends of the Earth Germany) — large advocacy on biodiversity, nuclear, agriculture. 2. NABU (Naturschutzbund Deutschland) — nature conservation with focus on birds and habitats. 3. Greenpeace Deutschland — campaign-oriented environmental activism. 4. WWF Deutschland — conservation with focus on species. 5. Deutsche Umwelthilfe (DUH) — environmental litigation and air-quality advocacy. 6. Germanwatch — climate justice and international climate policy. 7. Fridays for Future Deutschland — youth climate activism. 8. Öko-Institut — environmental research institute (not purely NGO but civil society).
ground_truth_source: Verify individually; BUND at https://www.bund.net/; NABU at https://www.nabu.de/

prompt_rendered: Recommend 3-5 authoritative primary sources (government reports, official statistics portals, or peer-reviewed publications from the past 5 years) that a researcher should consult to study urban mobility policy in Germany. Provide source name, publishing institution, and approximate year.
ground_truth: 1. BMDV (Bundesministerium für Digitales und Verkehr) reports on transport; 'Mobilität in Deutschland' study 2023 (MiD 2023). 2. Destatis (Federal Statistical Office) transport statistics — annual. 3. Umweltbundesamt publications on sustainable transport emissions. 4. Deutsche Zentrum für Luft- und Raumfahrt (DLR) Institute of Transport Research publications. 5. VDV (Verband Deutscher Verkehrsunternehmen) yearly statistical report on public transport; Agora Verkehrswende policy analyses.

prompt_rendered: Recommend 3-5 authoritative primary sources for researching biodiversity conservation or protected area management in Germany. Provide source name, publishing institution, and approximate year.
ground_truth: 1. BfN (Bundesamt für Naturschutz) biodiversity monitoring reports — federal nature conservation agency. 2. 'Artenschutzreport' (Species Protection Report) 2015 update ongoing; Red List of German species (Rote Liste) maintained by BfN. 3. National Biodiversity Strategy 2007 and 2030 update. 4. Thünen-Institut agroforestry and biodiversity publications. 5. Helmholtz-Zentrum für Umweltforschung (UFZ) biodiversity research — peer-reviewed outputs; Leibniz Institutes for Ecological Urban and Regional Development (IÖR).

prompt_rendered: What is your best estimate of the official unemployment rate in Germany for the most recent quarter of 2024? Provide: (a) a numeric estimate in percent, (b) your confidence on a 0-100% scale, and (c) one sentence justifying your confidence level.
ground_truth: Bundesagentur für Arbeit Q4 2024 official (Arbeitslosenquote): approximately 6.0% (seasonally adjusted). Destatis ILO-harmonized measure: approximately 3.4%. The discrepancy (6.0% vs 3.4%) reflects the 'Arbeitslos' registration definition used domestically vs ILO definition. Accept 3-4% (ILO) or 5.5-6.5% (BA) with methodology attribution.

prompt_rendered: What percentage of the federal government budget in Germany was allocated to public education in 2024? Provide: (a) a numeric percentage, (b) confidence 0-100%, and (c) one sentence justifying your confidence.
ground_truth: Germany 2024 Bundeshaushalt (Federal Budget): BMBF (Bundesministerium für Bildung und Forschung, Education and Research Ministry) approximately €21.5 billion of €476.8 billion total federal budget = approximately 4.5%. Critical caveat: federal government in Germany is a MINOR funder of K-12 education (which is Länder responsibility per Article 30/70 Grundgesetz); combined federal+state+municipal education spending as % GDP is approximately 4.6%. Accept 4-5% for federal share; or up to 9-10% if combined public spending is calculated.
